sentences.text: My name is Gideon Frieder, or Gidon Frieder, the way I pronounce it.
I was born in Zvolen, in Slovakia.
I was born on the 30th of September, 1937.
Yes.
I-- I was the first child.
My mother and father lived in Zvolen.
My father's name was Armin [?
Avraham ?] [?
Abba ?] Frieder.
He was the rabbi in Zvolen.
And my mother's name was Ruzena or [?
Rogi ?] Frieder.
Her maiden name was Berl, B-E-R-L.
I was the firstborn, followed three years later by my sister.
Her name was Gita Frieder.
Yes.
The maternal-- both the maternal and the fraternal grandparents were alive.
There was extended family on both sides.
My father had-- had a-- a brother, which is still alive.
And my mother had sisters and brothers.
Most of them died just-- just very recently.
One of them is still alive.
My father's brother who's still alive, his name is Emmanuel.
And he lives in Israel, in Netanya.
No, not to my knowledge.
The fraternal grandparents came from Prievidza.
And I assume that he lived there or-- or around there.
I would say around one-- two, three years old, or something of this nature.
I do not recall anything about Zvolen, except the fact that my-- that I was born there.
My first recollections are from our residence, in Nove Mesto.
My father became a rabbi in Nove Mesto.
Miraculously, the certificate of his appointment is still available.
And I have it with me.
I have it here today in my house.
It's framed.
He was a rabbi in Nove Mesto.
I don't remember the date, although that is recorded on the document, but before my recollection, of anything about myself.
No.
But we can ascertain it from the document.
I don't recall it as we speak.
But it had to be in the '40s, beginning of 40's.
I was born at the end of '37.
About that.
Well, I remember that we lived in a large house.
Although for a child, everything seems large.
Recently, my visit, I just couldn't make myself go back to Nove Mesto.
So I didn't see the house.
But people reported it was called the Hertzegova Villa, or the Villa of the Hertzog, of the Duke, the local nobleman.
It has a large garden.
There were animals in the garden.
I remember playing with a-- a little sheep in the garden.
There was a fence around the garden, which I remember.
I remember vividly, there were no paved roads.
And I remember that opposite that house, there was a smaller house, either grocery or some kind of food place.
Because I was fond going down into the cellar, which had permanent ice.
There were no refrigerators at that time.
But cellars, in Slovakia, if they were deep enough, had permanent ice.
Yeah.
And there were food-- food stuff there.
I remember going to the synagogue with my father, and sitting on his lap on the mizrah, so-called, on the-- facing the-- the congregation.
That's how in Orthodox, in Neolog synagogues, really, things were going on.
I recall later on going to school, first grade.
It was a Jewish school.
It was a regular first grade Jewish school for Jews only.
I recall very well that I had to walk.
And at certain roads, I wouldn't walk through.
Because the kids would beat me, because I was Jewish.
So I wouldn't go through certain neighborhoods to go home.
The-- the straight, quick road, was dangerous for me.
So I didn't go there.
I'd walk by myself.
I don't recall that any other children lived in the direction that I walked.
I wouldn't call it understanding.
I would call it awareness of the fact that Christians would beat Jews.
I didn't attribute it to any anti-Semitism in that sense.
I just know that Christians beat Jews.
I don't recall that I complained.
I think that for them, it was clear that Christians would beat Jews.
They just seen to it that I don't go through that neighborhood.
I sometimes was stubborn enough to go.
And sometimes I went through.
And sometimes I was beaten.
It-- I did not conceive it to be life-threatening, in that sense.
I don't think I had a conception of threat of life throughout that period.
No, it was beating.
It was shoving.
It was not beating with-- with clubs.
Or-- it was children, more or less my age, a bit older.
It was shoving and beating, and bloodying your knees, and your nose, and falling down, and scraping, and-- on that level.
I don't remember any friends.
I remember that-- that we went to families that there were children there.
But I cannot recall anybody by name.
I met somebody who-- whose parents were apparently friends of my parent.
And the parent, I stayed in his house, is today, the curator of the Jewish museum in Bratislava.
And he told me about the recollections that his father told him, not-- not his own.
[INAUDIBLE].
But I don't recall any friends from that time.
I couldn't tell you a name, for example.
I couldn't even tell you how big the class was.
I tried to recall it, but I can't.
It was a Jewish school.
It was not a public school.
Oh, I-- I know for a fact that I was learning how to read and write, because I was a voracious reader.
Slovak.
No.
There was only Slovak and-- and Hebrew.
And I was able to read Hebrew and Slovak.
Although I wouldn't-- I wasn't able to understand the Hebrew.
It was just reading, for prayer and writing.
They spoke fluently Slovak, Hungarian, and German.
In the house, they spoke all three languages.
And I picked up some German.
None of it-- of the-- the German I picked up even more after the war.
But I was cognizant of the three languages as I was very young.
And people just switch between the languages.
Outside we always spoke Slovak.
I-- I really cannot tell you.
I think it was very natural to speak Slovak when everybody spoke Slovak.
And between them, I interpreted that they switched languages so I will no understand.
I did not attribute to it any value at the time, at least.
I didn't even know about the Austro-Hungarian Empire and, you know, the heritage of languages that people had.
But being multilingual, was for me, a very natural thing.
I recall it as a large synagogue.
But again, the recollection of a small child, about size, is very misleading.
I also recall my-- my father being a huge man.
But I don't think he was.
Because seeing my-- my uncle, I think that my brother-- my uncle had approximately the same body structure.
He's not a tall man.
But as a child, I considered him a giant.
I did not follow up that question.
The-- the population was large.
Because there was a place called Ohel David, Ohel David, which was an old age home, which was used to shelter Jews from various prosecution.
And that increased-- inflated a bit, the size of the population, I think.
It was large enough for me to think it's a large congregation.
There were lots of people there on Shabbat evening and-- and holidays.
And for me, it was the whole city.
I know that-- that children ran around.
And it was a large, large gathering, for a small child.
Yes.
Status quo onto the Neolog yes.
He was in the status quo ante, but he-- he was on the Orthodox side of the status quo ante.
He was Orthodox side of the Neolog.
And in the book that was written about him, his struggle to orthodoxize, so to speak, to make more conservative the Neolog movement, are very evident.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was the 'Conservadox' type of thing in American terms, if you want.
I'm sure there were, but I was not aware of them.
At the time I was not aware of any dichotomy, or any tension in Judaism.
Later on, it became apparent to me.
I think she was born in Nove Mesto.
She was three years younger than I am.
We were ascertained from the dates of the appointment, when it was done.
No, she did not.
She was too young at that time.
She was too young.
She died very young.
The maternal grandparents eventually was-- were brought to Nove Mesto to [?
Eldavit. ?] So I met them frequently.
His name was Max Berl.
Her name was Hermina Berl.
He died during the Holocaust.
She survived the Holocaust.
I don't recall exactly.
I would estimate about 10 years, because she did come to Israel.
And I had the opportunity to spend time with her, in Israel.
Yes, in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was absent a long time.
And eventually, we had to flee.
Also-- also at the end of '43, '44, I visited with my father many times in various concentration camps.
These were not concentration camps in the sense of Germany.
These were-- they were called working camps, more the detention and work, than-- than concentration.
And I-- I recall that I was still with my father and couldn't understand very well why these people are there and not in homes.
But nobody bothered to explain it to me.
No.
We just came to visit, because he was a rabbi.
And he visited many places.
I didn't know that he was part of the working group.
That word was not even mentioned.
I became aware of it only after that war.
That working group, or [SLOVAK], as they call them in Slovak, was kind of an underground government of the Slovak Jewry.
It consisted of representative of various branches of Judaism, including the Orthodox, the Zionist movement.
The names I recall, [?
Giza ?] [?
Fleischman, ?] [?
Dovais ?] [?
Mendel, ?] [INAUDIBLE], my father, and two others.
They are in the book.
I just don't recall the name off-hand.
I-- until very recently, as a matter of fact, until the time that my uncle wrote the book about my father, I didn't go back to these memories.
They-- they were not something I touched.
I didn't talk about them.
I didn't-- my-- my wife, for many years, we married in 1960, so married many years now.
Although she knew I'm a survivor, she never read my story, so to speak.
She never inquired.
So I didn't dwell on-- on names and recollections.
I didn't read the book I gave you.
No.
I can't.
The name of the book in Hebrew is, [HEBREW].
It was translated to English under the name,
And it was written by Emanuel.
It was written by Emanuel Frieder, my uncle, based on the diaries of my father, and based on his recollections, and-- and his work after my father died.
In Israel, I-- I can go back and look.
We-- I have the original book here.
I think the '80s.
It was translated also to Slovak, by the way.
Sorry to interrupt you.
Yes.
I recall at least once that we stayed overnight.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I don't know when I was not with him, how long he will stay.
No, I cannot tell you.
I-- I-- we came.
We usually went to a social occasion.
I remember clearly once, we went to possibly one of the festivals.
Because there was a celebration and there was a-- even a-- a-- a performance.
And I even remember a phrase out of the performance.
But I was too young, and too many things happened since then, which overshadow that experience.
Yes.
Sered, Vyhne, and one more, but I don't recollect.
These were all in Slovakia.
At the time already Slovakia, was separate from-- it was a separate country.
Yes, it was forced labor.
Not of my own recollection.
I read-- I mean, I read a lot about the history since then.
And it's all secondhand.
I mean, it's all in the history books.
Oh, the Slovaks.
The Slovak government cooperated clearly with the German government.
And there's voluminous documentation in my father's memoirs which are now in-- in Yad Vashem, voluminous documents showing the-- the clear cooperation.
I would even say the willingness and eagerness of the Slovaks.
I could not tell you that.
No.
But the-- the numbers are documented very well in those memoirs.
As I said, it's part of the archives of Yad Vashem.
Not in Nove Mesto.
As a matter of fact, there were no-- no Germans in Nove Mesto, to the best of my recollection.

I don't recall any German from Nove Meso And indeed, the-- and the German army really invaded, in force, into Slovakia, only at the opposite-- onset, not opposite-- the onset of the Slovak National Uprising.
'44 That was in 1944.
I wouldn't say it was clear of trouble.
It was clear of Germans.
There were the Hlinka Guard, which was the Slovak equivalent of the SA, I would say, or the SS.
And they were clearly visible.
And it was clear that I should avoid them.
And everybody should avoid them, as much as one can.
There were Slovak gatherings that we had to avoid.
From my recollection, or from my knowledge?
Well, from my recollection as a child, the only thing I can tell you is being beaten by-- by the gentiles, so to speak, having no recourse to help, of any adult on the street.
If there was an adult, he or she would not interfere, clearly not on my behalf.
From my knowledge, the rules, the-- the legislature in Slovakia, was following pretty much a Nuremberg legislature.
They were willing participants.
I have quotes from Tiso.
Tiso was the leader of Slovakia [? then. ?] I would not call him the Fuhrer of Slovakia, but he was the leader of Slovakia.
He was a Catholic priest.
And there are many quotes from him, supporting the legislature.
He was clearly a willing participant.
His government was full of willing participants.
It was clear economic value, to the Slovaks, in depriving the Jews.
And in some quotes of Tiso, he specifically said there's nothing bad that the Jews will give back to Slovak what they took get away from them.
Although, you know, doing well economically doesn't mean that you take back from anybody.
But that was the attitude.
I had a tremendous amount of bitterness against the Slovaks.
For their willingness to participate as quislings, as people who follow.
It was not-- I don't justify the Germans by anyway.
But it's a very different story.
The Germans are guilty in a different way.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Slovaks did it from pure hatred.
And I think they continue doing it.
Well, Slovakia is a very strongly Catholic country.
The Catholic Church was never pro-Jewish.
So although the history in-- in Europe, even till this day, their passion plays in Germany, just clear anti-Semitic.
And in other places, Europe was always anti-Semitic.
I would even say they got it with their mother's milk.
I am [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very bitter about the fact that the Catholic Church promoted and supported such approach, and other parts of Christianity did not stand up against it.
And there are places which did stand up.
And we-- and we all know about the Danes, and the Dutch.
And-- that was not the story of all Europe.
I-- I had a Dane co-worker, which-- which said that the Danes-- how did he say it?
The Danes are ashamed that they didn't do enough.
What an irony.
It followed-- it followed the German-- it followed the German model.
Jewish businesses were identified, were robbed, and not protected.
Gentiles were asked not to buy from Jews, who are the suckers of the blood of the Slovak nation, the regular type of German copying.
The Hlinka Guard was very eager to-- to be [? as ?] perceived as strong as SA or the SS.
It just followed the same patterns, nothing innovative, nothing new.
Concentration camp, deportations, the Slovaks were paid for everybody who was deported.
And they were willing participants in it.
Yes.
Jews had to wear yellow stars.
I had a yellow star.
Oh, yes.
I had a small yellow star, because I was a child.
My parents had a bigger yellow star.
No.
No.
He was the rabbi.
There's a whole set of books on-- on his deeds.
And I don't think we can-- we can summarize it here, for two reasons.
A, it's lengthy, and B, I avoided reading those things.
Suffice it to say, that in the Jewish Museum, in Bratislava, now on the Slovak non-Communist government, there's a large display of his work with his name and-- and portrait and pictures.
He was awarded a-- he was awarded various honors by the Slovak pre-Communist government and by Jewish organizations.
There was a large forest, of well above 1,000 trees, planted in his name, in Israel, by the survivors.
His name is in the Jewish encyclopedia, not a big article, but an article mentioning him.
And as I said, it's very, very well-documented in-- in his memoirs.
[SIGHS] One thing he did, he stayed.
He had the opportunity to leave.
He-- he had a certificate to go to then Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He could have been alive today.
In the-- in the end of the '30s.
He-- he was part of the Zionist movement, very unusual for rabbis at the time, to be part of the Zionist movement.
Although, the Neologs were better in that sense.

But he was a strong part of the Zionist movement, and he-- he had the opportunity to leave and go to Israel, well, then Palestine, under British mandate, since the First World War, till the end of the First World War.
It was very difficult to got those certificates, to go legally.
But he had-- he decided to stay, because he was shepherding his flocks, so to speak.
